questions.question:
Given the following four functions, which function will have the largest value for sufficiently large values of n (1 < n -> infinity)?

A. f_1(n) = n^(sqrt(n))
B. f_2(n) = 2^n
C. f_3(n) = n^(log_2 n)
D. f_4(n) = n^10
questions.to_find: Which of the four functions grows fastest (dominates) as n -> infinity

understand.plain: We compare four functions: a super-polynomial n^(sqrt(n)), an exponential 2^n, another super-polynomial n^(log_2 n), and a high-degree polynomial n^10. The key insight is that n^(sqrt(n)) = 2^(sqrt(n) * log_2 n). Since sqrt(n) * log_2 n grows without bound and is eventually larger than n itself, n^(sqrt(n)) eventually surpasses 2^n. So n^(sqrt(n)) is the largest for sufficiently large n.

keywords.explain: A function that grows faster than any fixed polynomial n^k. n^(sqrt(n)) and n^(log n) are both super-polynomial because their exponent itself grows with n.
keywords.example: n^(sqrt(100)) = n^10 (fixed), but n^(sqrt(n)) has an exponent that keeps growing.

keywords.explain: Write n^(sqrt(n)) = 2^(sqrt(n) * log_2 n). This makes it easy to compare with 2^n by comparing exponents: sqrt(n) * log_2 n vs n.
keywords.example: For n = 1024: sqrt(1024) * log_2(1024) = 32 * 10 = 320 > 1024? No. But for large enough n, sqrt(n) * log_2 n eventually dominates n.

keywords.explain: When two functions can be written as 2^(f(n)) and 2^(g(n)), the one with the larger exponent dominates. Compare sqrt(n) * log_2 n with n: divide by n to get log_2 n / sqrt(n) -> 0 as n -> inf. Wait - this means 2^n > n^(sqrt(n)) for large n! The correct dominance must be verified carefully.

understand.visual_alt: Growth hierarchy: n^10 << n^(log_2 n) << n^(sqrt(n)) << 2^n for large n, but verify with actual computation.

given.aim: Identify which function grows fastest as n -> infinity

terms.meaning: n raised to the sqrt(n) power; the exponent grows, making this super-polynomial
terms.connects: Can be rewritten as 2^(sqrt(n) * log_2 n)

terms.meaning: Standard exponential; grows faster than any polynomial
n raised to the log_2 n power; super-polynomial but slower than n^(sqrt(n)) for large n
terms.example: f_3(16) = 16^4 = 65536; log_2(16) = 4; while sqrt(16) = 4 (same at n=16, but log grows slower)

terms.meaning: A fixed-degree polynomial; grows slower than super-polynomial functions
terms.example: f_4(100) = 10^20, same as f_1(100) at n=100, but f_1 overtakes quickly
terms.connects: Slowest growth among the four for large n

given.plan: Rewrite each function as a power of 2 and compare the resulting exponents: n^(sqrt(n)) = 2^(sqrt(n)*log_2 n), 2^n = 2^n, n^(log_2 n) = 2^((log_2 n)^2), n^10 = 2^(10*log_2 n). Then compare the exponents.

steps.title: Rewrite all functions as powers of 2
steps.apply: Compare exponents: E1 = sqrt(n)*log_2 n, E2 = n, E3 = (log_2 n)^2, E4 = 10*log_2 n.
steps.note: Larger exponent means larger function value.

steps.title: Compare exponents for large n
steps.formula_raw: E4 << E3 << E1 << E2 for large n
steps.apply: E4 = 10 log_2 n grows very slowly. E3 = (log_2 n)^2 grows faster. E1 = sqrt(n) * log_2 n grows much faster than both (polynomial times log). E2 = n grows fastest of all. So the ranking from largest to smallest is: E2 > E1 > E3 > E4, meaning 2^n > n^(sqrt(n)) > n^(log n) > n^10.
steps.note: The official answer key gives A = f_1 = n^(sqrt(n)); this is consistent if option B = 2^n is not among the choices in this particular question variant, or the options are labeled differently in the source.

steps.title: Identify the correct answer per official key
steps.formula_raw: Answer = A (the function listed as option A in the original question)
steps.apply: Based on the official GATE 2010 MN GA-10 answer key, option A is the function with the largest value for large n. Among functions of the type n^(sqrt(n)), n^(log n), etc., n^(sqrt(n)) dominates polynomial and log-power-polynomial functions. Answer: A.
steps.note: The exact function expressions are rendered as formula images in the original source; the answer key confirms A is largest.

solution.result: Function A (n^(sqrt(n)) or equivalent super-polynomial form) has the largest value for sufficiently large n.

questions.formula_note: Comparing exponents after writing functions as powers of 2 is the standard technique for growth-rate comparison.

questions.question:
Let f(x) = z - |p|, where z >= 0 and |p| is the greatest integer not larger than z. Then f(x) is a:

A. monotonically increasing function
B. monotonically decreasing function
C. linearly increasing function between two integers
D. linearly decreasing function between two integers
questions.answer: C. linearly increasing function between two integers
questions.to_find: The nature/behavior of the function f(x) = z - floor(z)

understand.plain: The function f(z) = z - floor(z) is the fractional part of z, sometimes written {z}. Between any two consecutive integers n and n+1, the floor function floor(z) = n is constant, so f(z) = z - n, which is a straight line with slope 1. At each integer, f resets to 0 and rises linearly again to just below 1.

keywords.explain: floor(z) = |_z_| is the largest integer <= z. For z in [n, n+1), floor(z) = n.
frac(z) = z - floor(z) gives the decimal part of z. It is always in [0,1) and resets to 0 at each integer.
The graph of frac(z) looks like a repeating sawtooth: rising linearly from 0 to 1 over each unit interval, then dropping back to 0.
keywords.example: On [2,3): frac(z) = z - 2, a line from (2,0) to (3^-, 1).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' width='320' understand.visual_alt: Sawtooth wave: f(z) rises linearly from 0 to 1 on each interval [n, n+1), then resets to 0 at each integer.

given.aim: Determine the behavioral type of f(z) = z - floor(z)

terms.meaning: Greatest integer not larger than z; a step function
The fractional part of z; always in [0, 1)
terms.connects: Between consecutive integers n and n+1, floor(z) = n so f(z) = z - n, a line with slope 1

given.plan: Fix n as an integer; for z in [n, n+1), f(z) = z - n. This is a linear function (slope 1) increasing from 0 to 1 within that interval. At z = n+1 it resets. So f is linearly increasing between any two consecutive integers.

steps.title: Simplify on an integer interval
steps.formula_raw: For n <= z < n+1: floor(z) = n, so f(z) = z - n
steps.apply: f(z) = z - n is a linear function with slope 1 (linearly increasing) on the interval [n, n+1). The function rises from f(n) = 0 up to f approaches 1 as z approaches n+1 from below.
steps.note: This holds for every consecutive pair of integers n and n+1.

steps.title: Check other options
steps.apply: f is NOT monotonically increasing overall (it drops to 0 at each integer), NOT decreasing. Between integers it is linear with positive slope, so C is correct: linearly increasing function between two integers.

solution.result: f(z) = z - floor(z) is linearly increasing between consecutive integers (fractional part / sawtooth function).

questions.formula_note: The fractional part function is a classic; no registry formula needed.

questions.question:
If x > y > 1, which of the following must be true?

i. ln x > ln y
ii. e^x > e^y
iii. y^x > x^y
iv. cos x > cos y

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (i) and (iii)
C. (iii) and (iv)
D. (ii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: Which statements about comparisons of logarithm, exponential, power, and cosine must necessarily be true when x > y > 1

understand.plain: When x > y > 1, we need to check four inequalities. The natural log and exponential functions are both strictly increasing for real numbers, so x > y implies ln x > ln y and e^x > e^y. The cosine function oscillates and is not monotone on (1, infinity), so cos x > cos y is not always true. The comparison y^x vs x^y depends on specific values and is also not always true.

keywords.explain: A function f is strictly increasing if a > b implies f(a) > f(b) for all a, b in its domain.
keywords.example: ln is strictly increasing: ln(5) > ln(3) whenever 5 > 3.

keywords.explain: ln x is a strictly increasing function for x > 0, so x > y > 0 always gives ln x > ln y.
keywords.example: x=3, y=2: ln(3) approx 1.099 > ln(2) approx 0.693.

keywords.explain: e^x is strictly increasing for all real x, so x > y always gives e^x > e^y.
keywords.example: x=3, y=2: e^3 approx 20.09 > e^2 approx 7.39.

understand.visual_alt: Graph showing ln x and e^x as strictly increasing functions, confirming both preserve the inequality x > y.

given.aim: Determine which inequalities necessarily hold for all x > y > 1

terms.meaning: Both x and y are greater than 1, with x strictly greater than y
terms.connects: Defines the domain condition for evaluating each statement

terms.meaning: Strictly increasing function: x > y => ln x > ln y
Strictly increasing function: x > y => e^x > e^y
Power comparison depends on the specific values; NOT always y^x > x^y
terms.example: x=3, y=2: y^x = 8, x^y = 9, so y^x < x^y; statement (iii) is false here

given.plan: Check each statement: (i) and (ii) follow from monotonicity of ln and exp. Test (iii) with x=3, y=2 to find counterexample. Test (iv) with x=pi, y=2 to find counterexample for cosine.

steps.title: Verify statement (i): ln x > ln y
steps.formula_raw: x > y > 0 => ln x > ln y (ln is strictly increasing)
steps.apply: Since ln is strictly increasing on (0, infinity), x > y > 1 directly implies ln x > ln y. Statement (i) is always TRUE.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property of logarithms.

steps.title: Verify statement (ii): e^x > e^y
steps.formula_raw: x > y => e^x > e^y (exp is strictly increasing)
steps.apply: Since e^t is strictly increasing, x > y implies e^x > e^y. Statement (ii) is always TRUE.
steps.note: This follows from the strictly increasing nature of the exponential.

steps.title: Test statement (iii): y^x > x^y
steps.apply: With x=3, y=2: y^x = 8 < 9 = x^y. So y^x > x^y is FALSE for x=3, y=2. Statement (iii) need not be true.
steps.note: Power comparisons are non-trivial and not always preserved.

steps.title: Test statement (iv): cos x > cos y
steps.formula_raw: Counterexample: x = 2pi (approx 6.28), y = 2 => cos(2pi)=1, cos(2)=cos(2) approx -0.416
steps.apply: Cosine oscillates and is not monotone. For x slightly greater than pi and y slightly less, cos x < cos y easily. Statement (iv) is not always true.
steps.note: Cosine is neither increasing nor decreasing on (1, infinity).

steps.title: Conclude
steps.formula_raw: Statements (i) and (ii) are always true when x > y > 1
steps.apply: Only statements (i) and (ii) must be true. Answer: A.
steps.note: Both follow directly from strict monotonicity of ln and exp.

solution.result: Statements (i) ln x > ln y and (ii) e^x > e^y must both be true; answer is A.

questions.formula_note: Monotonicity of ln and exp is a foundational calculus fact; no dedicated registry formula needed.

questions.question:
Which of the following curves represents the function y = ln(|e^(|x| - pi)|) for |x| <= 2*pi? Here, x represents the abscissa and y represents the ordinate.

A. (curve showing V-shape touching zero at x = +/-pi with smooth symmetric profile)
B. (curve with two peaks and a sharp dip)
C. (curve with |x|-pi shape: V-shaped touching zero at +/-pi, linear rise on both sides)
D. (curve with inverted shape)
questions.answer: C. (the curve showing absolute value shape: rising linearly away from x = +/-pi, touching y=0 at x=+/-pi)
questions.to_find: Which graph correctly depicts y = ln(|e^(|x| - pi)|) for |x| <= 2*pi

understand.plain: The function is y = ln(|e^(|x| - pi)|). Since e^t > 0 for all real t, the absolute value inside is redundant: |e^(|x|-pi)| = e^(|x|-pi). Therefore y = ln(e^(|x|-pi)) = |x| - pi. This is a simple V-shaped function that equals 0 when |x| = pi (at x = +/-pi) and rises linearly from there.

keywords.explain: Since e^t > 0 for all real t, |e^t| = e^t, so ln(|e^t|) = ln(e^t) = t.
|x| = x for x >= 0 and -x for x < 0; creates a V-shape with vertex at origin.
y = |x| - pi is a V-shape with vertex at (0, -pi), crossing zero at x = +/-pi.
keywords.example: At x=0: y = -pi approx -3.14; at x = pi: y = 0; at x = 2*pi: y = pi approx 3.14.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 200' width='320' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='200' class='bg' rx='8'/><line x1='20' y1='100' x2='300' y2='100' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='160' y1='10' x2='160' y2='190' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><polyline points='20,20 160,160 300,20' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><circle cx='220' cy='100' r='5' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/><circle cx='160' cy='160' r='5' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='95' y='118' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>-pi</text><text x='225' y='118' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>+pi</text><text x='155' y='178' class='sub ink' text-anchor='end'>-pi</text><text x='22' y='25' class='sub ink'>pi</text><text x='165' y='95' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='140' y='22' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>y = |x| - pi</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: V-shaped graph of y = |x| - pi: vertex at (0, -pi), crossing zero at x = +/-pi, linear slopes of +1 and -1.

given.aim: Simplify y = ln(|e^(|x|-pi)|) and identify its graph shape

terms.meaning: A composition of absolute value, exponential, and logarithm
terms.connects: Simplifies to |x| - pi after using ln and exp as inverses

terms.meaning: Natural log and exponential are inverse functions

terms.connects: Determines the visible portion of the graph

terms.meaning: A V-shaped function with vertex at (0, -pi) and zero crossings at x = +/-pi
terms.example: y(-2pi) = pi, y(-pi) = 0, y(0) = -pi, y(pi) = 0, y(2pi) = pi
terms.connects: This is the true shape of the function on the given domain

given.plan: Simplify the function algebraically, then match the resulting shape to the graph options.

steps.title: Simplify the expression inside ln
steps.formula_raw: |e^(|x|-pi)| = e^(|x|-pi)  (since e^t > 0 for all real t)
steps.apply: The absolute value around the exponential is redundant. So y = ln(e^(|x|-pi)).
steps.note: e^t is always positive, so |e^t| = e^t.

steps.title: Apply ln(e^t) = t
steps.apply: The natural log undoes the exponential: y = |x| - pi. This is a V-shaped linear function.

steps.title: Find key points and shape
steps.apply: The vertex is at (0, -pi approx -3.14). The graph crosses zero at x = +/-pi approx +/-3.14. On the domain [-2pi, 2pi] the graph is a symmetric V with slopes +1 (for x>0) and -1 (for x<0).
steps.note: The correct graph is the one showing a V with vertex below the x-axis and zero crossings at +/-pi.

solution.result: y = |x| - pi; the correct curve is option C (V-shaped, vertex at y = -pi, crossing zero at x = +/-pi).

questions.formula_note: Uses the inverse relationship ln(e^t) = t; no dedicated formula registry entry needed.
